perturbation.original_text: The Parties will cause their Representatives to observe the terms of this letter agreement, and they will be responsible for any breach of this agreement by any of their Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties will encourage their Representatives to observe the terms of this letter agreement, and they may be responsible for a breach of this agreement by their Representatives, contingent on the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'will cause' to 'will encourage' weakens the obligation, and replacing 'will be responsible' with 'may be responsible, contingent on the circumstances' introduces significant ambiguity regarding liability for breaches by Representatives. This makes the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-321 addresses agreements exempting lessors from liability for negligence. While this contract isn't a lease, the principle applies: weakening the responsibility for representatives' breaches introduces a condition where the parties might attempt to exempt themselves from liability for actions of their agents, creating an ambiguous and potentially unenforceable obligation, particularly if the 'circumstances' include the party's own negligence in supervising the representatives.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 3 Certain Prohibited Contracts and Provisions of Contracts previous SECTION 5-311 Certain agreements between husband and wife void up TITLE 3 Certain Prohibited Contracts and Provisions of Contracts next SECTION 5-322 Agreements exempting caterers and catering establishments from liability for negligence void and unenforceable This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-321 Agreements exempting lessors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-321. Agreements exempting lessors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable. Every covenant, agreement or understanding in or in connection with or collateral to any lease of real property exempting the lessor from liability for damages for injuries to person or property caused by or resulting from the negligence of the lessor, his agents, servants or employees, in the operation or maintenance of the demised premises or the real property containing the demised premises shall be deemed to be void as against public policy and wholly unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the parties' responsibility for breaches by their representatives, weakening the original obligation. The law explanation argues that this weakening could be interpreted as an attempt to exempt themselves from liability, which is addressed in NY General Obligations Law § 5-321. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that agreements exempting lessors from liability for negligence are void and unenforceable. While the contract isn't a lease, the law explanation argues the principle applies. By changing 'will be responsible' to 'may be responsible, contingent on the circumstances,' the contract creates a condition where the parties might attempt to avoid liability for the actions of their agents, which could include negligence. This introduces an ambiguity that could lead to unenforceability, therefore a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Parties or any of their Representatives are requested pursuant to, or required by, applicable law, regulation, rules of any national stock exchange, or legal process to disclose any of the Information, the Parties must notify each other promptly so that they may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy or, in the sole discretion of the party that initially furnished such Information, waive compliance with the terms of this letter agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Parties or any of their Representatives are requested pursuant to, or required by, applicable law, regulation, rules of any national stock exchange, or legal process to disclose any of the Information, the Parties should consider notifying each other, allowing them to seek a protective order or other remedy if deemed necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'must notify' with 'should consider notifying' makes the notification process discretionary, contradicting the original intention of a mandatory alert. The phrase 'if deemed necessary' adds further ambiguity, as the criteria for necessity are undefined.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation FD requires fair disclosure of material information to all investors simultaneously. By making the notification discretionary, the modified text opens the door for selective disclosure, contradicting the principles of Regulation FD. The 'if deemed necessary' clause allows a party to potentially avoid notifying the other, leading to potential selective disclosures that violate the SEC's fair disclosure rule.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: st or confidence for purposes of the "misappropriation" theory of insider trading under Section 10(b) of the Act and Rule 10b-5. The law of insider trading is otherwise defined by judicial opinions construing Rule 10b-5, and Rule 10b5-2 does not modify the scope of insider trading law in any other respect. (a) Scope of Rule . This section shall apply to any violation of Section 10(b) of the Act (15 U.S.C. 78j(b)) and § 240.10b-5 thereunder that is based on the purchase or sale of securities on the basis of, or the communication of, material nonpublic information misappropriated in breach of a duty of trust or confidence. (b) Enumerated "duties of trust or confidence." For purposes of this section, a "duty of trust or confidence" exists in the following circumstances, among others: (1) Whenever a person agrees to maintain information in confidence; (2) Whenever the person communicating the material nonpublic information and the person to whom it is communicated have a history, pattern, or practice of sharing confidences, such that the recipient of the information knows or reasonably should know that the person communicating the material nonpublic information expects that the recipient will maintain its confidentiality; or (3) Whenever a person receives or obtains material nonpublic information from his or her spouse, parent, child, or sibling; provided , however, that the person receiving or obtaining the information may demonstrate that no duty of trust or confidence existed with respect to the information, by establishing that he or she neither knew nor reasonably should have known that the person who was the source of the information expected that the person would keep the information confidential, because of the parties' history, pattern, or practice of sharing and maintaining confidences, and because there was no agreement or understanding to maintain the confidentiality of the information. 4. Part 243 is added to read as follows: PART 243 -- REGULATION FD Sec. 243.100 General rule regarding selective disclosure. 243.101 Definitions. 243.102 No effect on antifraud liability. 243.103 No effect on Exchange Act reporting status. Authority : 15 U.S.C. 78c, 78i, 78j, 78m, 78o, 78w, 78mm, and 80a-29, unless otherwise noted. § 243.100 General rule regarding selective disclosure . (a) Whenever an issuer, or any person acting on its behalf, discloses any material nonpublic information regarding that issuer or its securities to any person described in paragraph (b)(1) of this section, the issuer shall make public disclosure of that information as provided in § 243.101(e): (1) Simultaneously, in the case of an intentional disclosure; and (2) Promptly, in the case of a non-intentional disclosure. (b)(1) Except as provided in paragraph (b)(2) of this section, paragraph (a) of this section shall apply to a disclosure made to any person outside the issuer: (i) Who is a broker or dealer, or a person associated with a broker or dealer, as those terms are defined in Section 3(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S.C. 78c(a)); (ii) Who is an investment adviser, as that term is defined in Section 202(a)(11) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 (15 U.S.C. 80b-2(a)(11)); an institutional investment manager, as that term is defined in Section 13(f)(5) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S.C. 78m(f)(5)), that filed a report on Form 13F (17 CFR 249.325) with the Commission for the most recent quarter ended prior to the date of the disclosure; or a person associated with either of the foregoing. For purposes of this paragraph, a "person associated with an investment adviser or institutional investment manager" has the meaning set forth in Section 202(a)(17) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 (15 U.S.C. 80b-2(a)(17)), assuming for these purposes that an institutional investment manager is an investment adviser; (iii) Who is an investment company, as defined in Section 3 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 (15 U.S.C. 80a-3), or w
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the mandatory notification requirement, creating a potential conflict with Regulation FD. The original text states "the Parties must notify each other promptly," while the changed text says they "should consider notifying each other," making the notification discretionary. This discretionary language, combined with "if deemed necessary," contradicts the principle of fair disclosure, as it could lead to selective disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 2 includes "PART 243 -- REGULATION FD Sec. 243.100 General rule regarding selective disclosure" which, though not explicitly defining the requirements, sets the stage for the regulation. Given that the change weakens a mandatory disclosure, there is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If COMPANY or COMSYS determines not to proceed with the Transaction, they will promptly inform the other Party of that decision and, in that case, and at any time upon request of COMPANY or COMSYS or any of their Representatives, the other party will either (i) promptly destroy all copies of the written Information in its or its Representatives’ possession and confirm such destruction to the initiating company in writing or (ii) promptly deliver to the initiator of such action all copies of the written Information in its or its Representatives’ possession.
perturbation.changed_text: If COMPANY or COMSYS determines not to proceed with the Transaction, they should endeavor to inform the other Party of that decision. In that case, and upon request, the other party will make a reasonable effort to either (i) destroy copies of the written Information or (ii) deliver the information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will promptly inform' to 'should endeavor to inform' makes the notification of transaction termination optional and less time-sensitive. Furthermore, replacing 'will either (i) promptly destroy' and 'promptly deliver' with 'make a reasonable effort to either (i) destroy' or '(ii) deliver' weakens the obligation to destroy or return information, and introduces the subjective term 'reasonable effort.'
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 3103
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly applicable, NY CPLR § 3103 concerns protective orders in discovery and emphasizes the court's power to prevent abuse. The original clause ensured the destruction or return of confidential information. The modified version weakens that assurance, potentially leading to disputes later if litigation ensues related to the failed transaction and information control becomes critical. The 'reasonable effort' standard becomes open to interpretation and could be argued as insufficient in preventing improper disclosure, potentially creating grounds for seeking court intervention under CPLR § 3103 to ensure adequate protection of confidential information.

Protective orders. (a) Prevention of abuse. The court may at any time on its own initiative, or on motion of any party or of any person from whom or about whom discovery is sought, make a protective order denying, limiting, conditioning or regulating the use of any disclosure device. Such order shall be designed to prevent unreasonable annoyance, expense, embarrassment, disadvantage, or other prejudice to any person or the courts. (b) Suspension of disclosure pending application for protective order. Service of a notice of motion for a protective order shall suspend disclosure of the particular matter in dispute. (c) Suppression of information improperly obtained. If any disclosure under this article has been improperly or irregularly obtained so that a substantial right of a party is prejudiced, the court, on motion, may make an appropriate order, including an order that the information be suppressed.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract language introduces ambiguity regarding the obligation to inform the other party and handle confidential information, contrasting with the need for clear directives. By changing 'will promptly inform' to 'should endeavor to inform', the notification becomes optional, and the replacement of 'will promptly destroy' and 'promptly deliver' with 'make a reasonable effort to either (i) destroy' or '(ii) deliver' weakens the obligation. While NY CPLR § 3103, as shown in the snippets, focuses on protective orders and prevention of abuse in discovery, it indicates the court's power to ensure adequate protection of information. The modified text's 'reasonable effort' standard is open to interpretation. Therefore, the original contract provided a stronger assurance of confidentiality, while the modification undermines this assurance.

perturbation.original_text: Neither Party hereto shall in any way or in any form disclose, publicize or advertise in any manner the discussions that give rise to this Agreement or the discussions or negotiations covered by this Agreement, including the fact that any such discussions have taken or are taking place, or any proposal relating thereto, including the terms and conditions thereof, without the prior written consent of the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither Party hereto should disclose, publicize or advertise the general nature of the discussions or negotiations covered by this Agreement without the prior consent of the other Party, such consent not to be unreasonably withheld.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'should' weakens the prohibition on disclosure. The addition of 'general nature' introduces ambiguity about what constitutes disclosable information. The phrase 'such consent not to be unreasonably withheld' creates a loophole, as 'unreasonably' is subjective and open to interpretation, potentially leading to disputes about whether consent was rightly withheld.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 prohibit manipulative and deceptive devices in the purchase or sale of securities. While this agreement is about a potential transaction, premature or misleading disclosure (even of a 'general nature') permitted under the altered clause, could be argued as manipulative if it affects market prices or investor decisions, and such disclosure might be argued as being 'unreasonable withholding of consent' to disclose under this change. This creates a conflict with the strict prohibitions of 10(b) and 10b-5 against actions that mislead investors.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The original contract strictly prohibits any disclosure related to the discussions or negotiations without prior written consent. The modified text weakens this prohibition by changing 'shall' to 'should,' introducing ambiguity with 'general nature,' and creating a loophole with 'such consent not to be unreasonably withheld.' This introduces an ambiguity because 'unreasonably withheld' is subjective and open to interpretation. According to the Law Explanation, premature or misleading disclosure (even of a 'general nature') permitted under the altered clause, could be argued as manipulative if it affects market prices or investor decisions. This aligns with 15 USC 78j, which prohibits 'manipulative and deceptive devices' related to securities exchanges, supporting the assessment that the changes create a legal contradiction by potentially allowing disclosures that could be considered manipulative.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby acknowledge that, unless and until a definitive agreement between COMPANY and COMSYS with respect to any Transaction has been executed and delivered, neither the COMPANY nor COMSYS will be under any legal obligation of any kind whatsoever with respect to such Transaction and hereby waive, in advance, any claims (including, without limitation, breach of contract) in connection with any Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties acknowledge that absent a definitive agreement, COMPANY and COMSYS may not be under any legal obligation. They generally waive any claims in connection with such Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will be under any legal obligation of any kind whatsoever' with 'may not be under any legal obligation' weakens the disclaimer of obligation. Removing 'in advance' from the waiver and replacing 'hereby waive...any claims' with 'generally waive any claims' makes the waiver less absolute and introduces uncertainty as to what claims are still preserved.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Statute of Frauds - General Obligations Law § 5-701
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-701 requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. By weakening the explicit disclaimer of obligation and making the waiver less absolute, the modified text could allow for arguments that an implied agreement existed, despite the lack of a definitive written contract. This creates ambiguity about whether the Statute of Frauds applies, and whether an oral agreement could be argued to exist, directly contradicting the original intent of a complete disclaimer of any obligation prior to a written agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the disclaimer of legal obligation and the waiver of claims, potentially conflicting with NY GOL § 5-701. The original text contains an explicit disclaimer, stating that neither party 'will be under any legal obligation of any kind whatsoever.' The changed text weakens this to 'may not be under any legal obligation,' suggesting a possibility of obligation where none was intended. Additionally, the original text includes a waiver 'in advance' of any claims, which is removed in the changed text. The original text 'hereby waive...any claims' becomes 'generally waive any claims,' making the waiver less absolute and creating uncertainty. NY GOL § 5-701 requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. By weakening the explicit disclaimer and the waiver, the modified text allows for arguments that an implied agreement existed, despite the lack of a definitive written contract as mentioned in the Law Explanation. The statute of frauds requires certain agreements to be in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged. Snippet 1 states, 'Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith...'. The modified text could be interpreted as opening the door to claims based on oral agreements or implied obligations, thereby contradicting the Statute of Frauds.
